title: It's Not Supposed to Be This Way

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
The mind excels at scripting how life ought to unfold. It insists you deserve daily happiness, with sadness appearing only as contrast. You want loved ones cheerful and problems absent. You'd erase "uncertain," "brutal," and "disappointing" from language if you could.

You carry expectations inside an uncontrollable world. Wanting the best is human; clinging to specific outcomes is natural. Yet nearly eight billion people — plus countless forces beyond you — leave little room for steering everything. We're woven into a larger fabric; hundreds of variables you never managed still aligned so you could eat breakfast today.

Sometimes you grab the wheel, then weather turns, night falls, and unplanned detours demand sharp turns. Everyone knows the ache of opening your hands to receive and catching bruises instead. Life rarely matches the blueprint. Don't fixate solely on outcomes that strayed from the plan.

Disappointment can become a dungeon or a greenhouse — your response decides.

Stay with this summary for guidance through letdowns. Lysa TerKeurst urges noticing what God has planted around you instead of staring at what's missing. Goodness persists; it simply needs your attention.

chapters.title: Ask why this hardship fits you
chapters.text:
People chase their best lives and reach for excellence. TerKeurst ties that impulse to Eden — a memory that perfection once felt attainable. Expecting flawlessness turns every result into failure because nothing qualifies.

We filter photos, fake smiles at parties we hate, and bristle when others miss our expectations. We measure reality against fantasies the mind manufactures, then feel disappointment and inadequacy. Don't fear letdown or bury it — disappointment exists so you can ask what to adjust to honor your hopes.

Feelings deserve expression — let them speak and steer you.

Yet emotions shouldn't be your only guide. Pain and unmet hopes can be God's method of teaching the lesson required before dreams materialize.

Hard seasons aren't punishments; they're curriculum. Listen for what pain teaches. Prioritize growth over how much it hurts. Disappointment can sharpen gratitude and clarify desire.

During her divorce, TerKeurst's world imploded — not just a twenty-five-year marriage ending, but an entire familiar life dissolving to dust with no path backward. Heartbreaking as that is, dust is God's preferred raw material for something new.

When she discovered her husband's affair, grief demanded the planet pause. In those depths she briefly believed she could outmanage God. Yet God pursues what's truly best, keeping you tethered to yourself and to Him.

Like lunar phases — beginning, peak, ending — people, status, careers, and health all cycle. The most devastating finale can clear space for a superior start.

chapters.title: Lean into the journey
chapters.text:
Can you honestly say, "What a perfect moment to shatter and grow"? Probably not — so don't resent God when hardship arrives uninvited. He builds toward your highest good rather than comfort or settling.

Rational in theory; brutal in practice when every cell screams and quitting feels as sensible as resigning from a miserable job.

No mantra or exercise instantly mends heartbreak — no pill heals a broken leg overnight. Recovery from trauma is a process.

The tunnel's only exit runs straight through. Healing begins by feeling the pain.

TerKeurst landed in critical care when her health collapsed. Seconds felt audible. She begged God to stop the agony; silence answered. Isn't God supposed to intervene in desperation? Where is He during war, genocide, a child's cancer death?

The pain, it turned out, saved her. It triggered tests, surgery, treatment — efforts that kept her alive. Physical or emotional anguish signals deeper change brewing. Intensity can erode trust in God, yet He aims to rescue and instruct, not harm or be ignored. Your task is to perceive the signal. Trust the wider vantage He holds.

Mid-storm, purpose often hides. Don't demand knowledge you're not ready to carry. Hunt meaning inside suffering. Drowning in unmet expectations can obscure why any of it happened. None of this is easy — survival means trusting God, honoring feelings, and extracting lessons.

chapters.title: Paint your own canvas
chapters.text:
When pain feels louder than the walls around you, you pray every problem vanishes. You can't exit hurt on command, but you can quit habits that drain you — creating room to heal and hear God.

TerKeurst unplugged from television and social media, choosing to receive rather than constantly give. Guard your warmth; don't burn it away for others. Before any action, ask whether it serves recovery; then decide.

Slow pace and patience are non-negotiable for healing.

Strength breeds confidence; vulnerability feels riskier because not everyone offers safe, accepting space. Control your emotional response and others' judgments lose leverage. When someone says, "I don't want to be with you," don't translate it as "I'm worthless." Parents rejecting your feelings doesn't invalidate them.

Don't fear yourself. Invite frightening thoughts and insecurities in, examine them, and define who you are independently. Others' opinions won't shatter you. You are beautiful, singular, loved, and enough. Discount barbs from unhappy people.

When plans collapse, light and laughter vanish, replaced by darkness and silence. The future you painted erases. A crash becomes blank canvas in life's book — you choose the colors. Don't let "not good enough" silence your artistry.

God wants you loved and free to create. The enemy wants you stuck in pain. Drowning in disappointment pushes you toward jobs you hate and hollow relationships. You may drop the brush and accept mediocrity you don't deserve.

chapters.title: Flaws fuel growth
chapters.text:
Humans are unfinished works; creation is our calling. Embracing imperfection breeds compassion — the ability to say, "I see you as you are, and I'm staying beside you."

Don't let perfectionism block authenticity or the person you're becoming.

If only everyone knew how radiant they look when real. Authenticity draws us to art — and to people we truly love. Witnessing someone express themselves through creation is magnetic: "That's me — flaws, strengths, scars, gifts. I'm free." You're here to taste every hue of experience. Travel the full spectrum without fear.

I don't want to let disappointment and heartbreak cause me to approach this life more cautious than creative. More critical than compassionate. More cynical than surrendered. ~ Lysa TerKeurst

Hard seasons resemble tightrope walking — perpetual balance between falling and advancing. Danger, pain, and shadow surround you, yet the path prepares you for purpose or reveals inner resources your dreams require. A joyful family, for instance, demands self-love first — no small task.

Some lessons are prerequisites for happiness ahead. When the load feels unbearable, remember God may send hardship as essential ingredients for a more beautiful life. Feeling weak or faithless during overwhelm is normal — use those signals for reflection, not surrender.

Scrutinize desires too; some originate from forces isolating you from God's truth. Mistakes — past or imminent — deserve honest light where love and wisdom still shine.

Did you know? A massive wave of crying, screaming, or panic often points to an unhealed wound.

chapters.title: You choose how to see it
chapters.text:
Life feels awkward because it is — also confusing, messy, unpredictable. Sudden turns catch you off guard. Craving stability and reassurance is natural, yet uncertainty can erode confidence.

Sometimes you must learn to breathe inside "I don't know," "If you love me, why pull away," "I'm in the wrong place," or "This makes no sense but I have no choice." Treat those moments as navigators — integral to who you're becoming. "I don't know" is profoundly human; it may steer you from knowledge too heavy right now.

Every pain carries purpose — stay curious about it.

Stay near God and peace fills the heart. You author your story. Others may spill ink on your canvas or strike through cherished sections, but your eyes and mind frame the meaning. Fill that frame with love, trust in God's plan, compassion, and patience.

Misreading your path often means misreading God's will. What if pain and disappointment open doors to your soul you'd never surrender? What if they sculpt your best self? God permits suffering but wants transformation — and promises companionship along the way. Notice the countless forms His presence takes.

Purpose inside pain is hard to spot. Feeling it doesn't mean you're failing. Perpetual "don't worry, be happy" is impossible. Let hurt move through you; don't camp there permanently. When ready, you can shift the narrative and glimpse joy even inside hardship.

To live is to love. To love is to risk pain. To risk pain is to live. It's what it means to truly be human. ~ Lysa TerKeurst

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Being human is complicated — vast inner and outer worlds colliding. Disappointment and suffering, though, forge character and shape who you aim to become. They're part of the road toward your calling.

Wounds prompt action. God uses them to highlight lessons needed for happiness and connection — to yourself and to Him. Practice compassion inward and outward so fewer people feel isolated in pain.

Avoiding hardship steals life's gifts; face it courageously. Each effort moves you toward what's best — like daily exercise where single steps compound into transformation.

Fill your heart with trust; God sees the panorama you can't. Gather strength to advance — you may be nearer the beginning than the end, meaning still hidden. Stay patient; let God guide you where you're meant to arrive. Be the artist on blank canvas — create. Turn heartbreak's dust into clay. Be the potter eager to build, anchored in love, trust, and connection to God.

Try this

Write a reflection answering these questions:

• What is there to learn from your hardship?
• What benefits can you gain from the place you are at?
• What are the small glimpses of joy in your struggles?
